metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Assign forced-BLOCK bridge ownership explicitly: either WS4 implements the abstain→BLOCK short-circuit stub (with WS5 activating it via `blocking_enabled=True`), or WS5 is resequenced to `depends_on WS4` and given explicit write authority over `produce_code_review_verdict`.
findings.finding: WS5's forced-BLOCK short-circuit is placed inside `produce_code_review_verdict` (a WS4-owned function), but WS5 declares only `depends_on WS3` — not WS4. Simultaneously, WS4 explicitly scopes detectors OUT. This creates both a dependency gap (WS5 needs WS4 to be finalized before it can insert the bridge) and an ordering violation (WS4 and WS5 can land concurrently on the same WS3 base, racing to modify the same function).
findings.location: child bba6-69b9-b9d4-4ac0 (WS5) + child 98ff-d3b8-d2f7-4a86 (WS4)
findings.suggested_fix: Add `depends_on WS4` to WS5 and assign bridge implementation to WS5 (post-WS4 extension). Alternatively, have WS4 implement the bridge as a consumer hook (callable by the verdict assembler if any detector criterion abstains), with WS5 registering the secrets/security criteria into that hook.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a short description of the 'force-enable override' mechanism to the Design or Decisions pinned section: what it enables (scope), who can invoke it, and its relationship to the fail-closed posture (e.g., does it bypass the BLOCK verdict or only the staged-rollout gate?).
findings.finding: The WS6 AC references a 'force-enable override' mechanism ('REWIRE d251's shipped review_bot.adapter.code_review_decision over produce_code_review_verdict ... with a fail-closed vote mapping + a coverage-gap-vs-finding message distinction + a force-enable override') but no corresponding description of this mechanism, its scope, or its safety contract appears anywhere in the plan body (Design, Decisions pinned, or Specifics pinned sections).
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — WS6 AC line; absent from Design and Decisions pinned sections

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a WS5 fixture (real or stubbed gitleaks SARIF 2.1.0 output) that exercises `sarif.from_sarif(trust_rebar_bag=False)` end-to-end through the detector envelope, verifying that secrets findings are correctly mapped to rebar findings with accurate locations — separate from the fail-closed posture test.
findings.finding: The plan asserts that gitleaks's SARIF 2.1.0 output is correctly parsed by the generic `sarif.from_sarif(trust_rebar_bag=False)` backend, but no specific empirical probe or fixture for this path is cited. The round-11 derisk experiment is cited for the semgrep/opengrep integration shape; gitleaks is described as emitting SARIF 2.1.0 and 'integrated via a generic SARIF-ingest backend' without a corresponding test that gitleaks's specific SARIF dialect is correctly ingested (field mapping, result schema, location anchors) by rebar's own ingest code.
findings.location: Decisions pinned section — 'Integration shape pinned by derisk experiment (Round-11)' / WS5 AC (bba6-69b9-b9d4-4ac0)
findings.suggested_fix: In WS5, add a unit test that feeds a known gitleaks SARIF 2.1.0 sample (e.g., captured from a canary run against a test repo with a planted fake secret) through `sarif.from_sarif(trust_rebar_bag=False)` and asserts the output finding has the correct `rule_id`, `location` (file+line), and `severity` fields that Pass-3 will consume for the auto-block decision.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `WS5` as an explicit `depends_on` prerequisite for WS6 (or justify in the sequencing section why the gate can safely operate without the fail-closed secrets detector during WS6's offline-verification phase).
findings.finding: WS6 (Gerrit rewire) is sequenced as `depends_on WS4` only, but WS5 (fail-closed secrets/security detectors) is NOT listed as a WS6 prerequisite. This means the four-pass gate could be wired into the live Gerrit voter and begin issuing verdicts before the fail-closed hard auto-block for secrets/High-Critical is installed, contradicting the design mandate that 'fail-CLOSED lives in THIS gate's Pass-3 consumer' and that secrets/security have a hard auto-block posture.
findings.location: Implementation sequencing (workstreams) — WS6 entry: 'depends_on WS4'; vs Design section: 'Fail-CLOSED lives in THIS gate's Pass-3 consumer (abstain on a secrets/High-Critical detector → BLOCK + coverage-gap)'
findings.suggested_fix: Change WS6 sequencing entry to 'depends_on WS4 + WS5', and add a note that the offline FakeRunner test for WS6 must include a fixture where the secrets detector is absent/errors and assert the resulting vote is BLOCK (not PASS).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit `depends_on WS5` to WS6 (or at minimum WS6-b) in the sequencing section and in WS6's AC, so the Gerrit voter cannot be activated (even in staged rollout) before the fail-closed secrets/security detectors are verified complete.
findings.finding: WS6 (Gerrit adapter rewire) has an explicit `depends_on WS4` but no dependency on WS5 (secrets/fail-closed detectors). Since WS4 and WS5 are parallel branches from WS3, the Gerrit voter can be activated (even in staged rollout) before the fail-closed secrets/High-Critical-security detectors are wired in. The gate's 'Capability' partition groups WS5 alongside WS1-WS4 as prerequisites, but the `depends_on` chain for WS6 does not enforce this, leaving a sequencing gap where the voter goes live without the hard auto-block path.
findings.location: Implementation sequencing (workstreams) — WS5 and WS6 dependency declarations
findings.suggested_fix: In the workstream list, change WS6's dependency to `depends_on WS4 + WS5`. If WS4 and WS5 need to remain parallel for speed, gate only WS6-b (live dogfood) on WS5, but require WS5's RED-fixture test to pass before the staged voter can issue any BLOCK votes.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Extend `code_review_verdict.schema.json` (WS4) to include a `security_detectors` sub-field in `coverage` (or a top-level `coverage_notes` list) carrying the abstain reason, and update WS5's bridge to populate it.
findings.finding: WS6's vote-mapping logic derives the `scanner` coverage-gap sub-reason from `verdict.coverage` containing a `security_detectors` note with `reason=='fail-closed-abstain'`, but WS4's `code_review_verdict` schema explicitly defines `coverage` as `{det_ms, llm_ms, total_ms, llm_calls, llm_ran}` — no `security_detectors` field. No sibling adds this field to the schema.
findings.location: child 4119-1a4f-19ff-4cfa (WS6) + child 98ff-d3b8-d2f7-4a86 (WS4)
findings.suggested_fix: Add `coverage_notes: [{detector_id, reason}]` (optional array) to `code_review_verdict.schema.json` in WS4; WS5 populates it with `{detector_id: 'gitleaks'|'semgrep', reason: 'fail-closed-abstain'|'detector-finding'}` when forcing BLOCK; WS6 reads from that field for sub-reason derivation.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Explicitly state in WS5's scope that it edits `criteria_routing.json` to flip `blocking_enabled` from `False` to `True` for the secrets and High-Critical-security criteria, and that WS2's test asserting `False` must be updated by WS5 to assert `True`.
findings.finding: WS5 says it sets `blocking_enabled=True` for the secrets/security criteria 'via WS2's resolver,' but using a resolver only READS values — changing `blocking_enabled` to `True` requires editing `criteria_routing.json` (a file authored/owned by WS2). WS5 does not explicitly state it edits `criteria_routing.json`, creating ambiguity about who makes the final edit and whether the shipped file has the correct value.
findings.location: child bba6-69b9-b9d4-4ac0 (WS5) + child 8e78-d515-3a98-4eba (WS2)
findings.suggested_fix: Add to WS5's scope: 'Edit `src/rebar/llm/code_review/criteria_routing.json` to set `blocking_enabled: true` for the secrets and High-Critical-security criteria keys (the WS2→WS5 handoff). Update or supersede WS2's test that asserts `blocking_enabled=False` for these keys.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `depends_on WS6-b` to WS7 (or make the live-apply AC explicitly conditional on WS6-b being closed), so the GitHub cutover cannot proceed until the voter has demonstrated at least one successful end-to-end dogfood run.
findings.finding: WS7's live cutover proof ('a change submitted in Gerrit replicates to GitHub main') functionally requires a working, vetted voter (WS6-b's dogfood proof), but WS7 only `relates_to WS6` — it has no `depends_on WS6-b`. If WS7's live apply is executed before WS6-b confirms the voter is healthy, `main` could be locked under an undertested voter gate.
findings.suggested_fix: Update WS7's Dependencies section: change 'relates_to WS6' to 'depends_on WS6-b (0da5) for the live-gated ACs; relates_to WS6 for the CONTRIBUTING.md and cutover plan (doable without live env).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Pin the selection/priority rule for overlay_union's cap enforcement (e.g., 'deterministic: catalog-index order among triggered overlays, with base-reviewer recommend_overlays appended after glob-triggered ones') so the WS3 implementer and FakeRunner test author have a single unambiguous rule.
findings.finding: The `overlay_union` op is specified to 'cap it (top-N)' when more than N overlays are triggered, but no ordering/priority rule is stated for selecting which N to keep — catalog order, confidence score from base reviewer, a priority field in the overlay descriptor, or some other rule. An agent implementing WS3's `overlay_union` scripted op must invent this rule, risking inconsistent behavior across implementations.
findings.location: Design section — 'union (uses: overlay_union)' workflow step + WS3 AC (crow-knelt-fiber)
findings.suggested_fix: Add one sentence to the 'Decisions pinned' or WS3 sections: 'Overlay selection order for cap: (1) deterministically-triggered overlays in catalog-index order, (2) base-reviewer-recommended overlays in recommend_overlays list order; first N=3 across both groups are dispatched, remainder dropped.' This makes overlay_union fully deterministic and the FakeRunner test authorable.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit WS6-b rollback procedure (e.g., 'deactivate the per-project Gerrit opt-in label config; voter returns to inert; no code change required') analogous to WS7's snapshot-restore step, and document it in WS6-b's ACs.
findings.finding: The WS6-b live voter activation lacks an explicit rollback procedure. The plan describes 'staged opt-in before fleet-wide enforcement' as a risk-mitigation posture, but does not state a concrete back-out step (e.g., 'deactivate the per-project opt-in flag; the voter returns to inert') for the scenario where the live four-pass voter issues wrong BLOCKs on real rebar changes. WS7 explicitly names a rollback snapshot; WS6-b has none. Because the single-pass path has been permanently deleted (one-way door), there is no prior behavior to fall back to — making an explicit disable procedure mandatory under T4.
findings.location: WS6-b AC (van-withe-folly) / 'Specifics pinned — WS6 staged rollout' section
findings.suggested_fix: In WS6-b's ACs, add: 'Rollback: set the per-project `LLM-Review` label `function` back to `NoOp` (or remove the project-level label config) in `All-Projects.config`; the voter immediately stops casting votes with no code deployment required. Verify: submit a test change and confirm no `LLM-Review` vote appears.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a doc pointer for `gates/code-review.yaml`'s schema to WS3's ACs (either a section in `docs/review-kernel.md` covering gate-config fields, or a companion `gates/code-review.schema.yaml`/README), so operators know the valid keys, value ranges, and semantics for cap/thresholds.
findings.finding: The new `gates/code-review.yaml` config artifact — which controls overlay cap (N=3), per-criterion block/advisory thresholds, and the two-round escalation shape — has no documented format, schema reference, or doc/ADR pointer in the plan. Operators and contributors who need to tune thresholds or add overlays have no specification to consult.
findings.location: WS3 AC (crow-knelt-fiber) / 'Overlay cap N = 3' decision in 'Decisions pinned' section
findings.suggested_fix: Extend `docs/review-kernel.md` with a 'Gate configuration' section that specifies: `overlay_cap` (int, default 3), `threshold_for.<criterion>` (float 0–1, block vs. advisory), and the escalation round semantics. Reference this doc in WS3's ACs.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Document the overlay catalog extension protocol (how to add/deprecate an overlay: enum entry, trigger config, prompt file, and `overlay_union` awareness) in an ADR or a `docs/overlay-catalog.md` section referenced from `CONTRIBUTING.md`.
findings.finding: The overlay catalog (the ~11 specialist reviewers) uses a FIXED ENUM to constrain `recommend_overlays`, but the plan provides no documented extension point or ADR guidance for how new overlays are added or deprecated after initial ship. The enum constraint is an intentional safety mechanism, but it means adding a new overlay is a multi-file code change (enum update, new prompt/trigger config, `overlay_union` logic awareness) with no stated process.
findings.location: Design section — 'overlay_id constrained to the FIXED catalog enum' / WS2 AC (8e78-d515-3a98-4eba)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define and document a rule-set update policy for the vendored semgrep rules (e.g., quarterly review cadence, a diff-review checklist, and a false-positive spike response procedure — such as temporarily raising the threshold or toggling to advisory) in `docs/security-detectors.md` or the WS5 child story.
findings.finding: The vendored semgrep/opengrep rule sets (`p/owasp-top-ten` + `p/cwe-top-25` subset) are described as 'vendored pinned' but the plan specifies no update/drift policy — no stated cadence for rule-set refresh, no mechanism for handling false-positive spikes introduced by upstream rule changes, and no owner for reviewing diffs when rules are bumped.
findings.location: Decisions pinned section — 'vendored pinned p/owasp-top-ten+p/cwe-top-25 subset, diff-scoped'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] WS6-b AC: add a minimum observability baseline — structured logging of vote decisions (PASS/BLOCK/error) and at least one CloudWatch alarm on the voter's error rate — as a done-criterion before fleet-wide rollout.
findings.finding: The plan has no observability requirement for the live review_bot voter service after WS6-b goes live — no logging, metrics, or alerting specified. The only success criterion for WS6-b is 'at least one real rebar change reviewed end-to-end', which is a one-time functional check rather than an ongoing operational baseline.
findings.location: WS6-b AC — 'LIVE dogfood: at least one real rebar change reviewed + gated end-to-end on the deployed AWS/Gerrit env'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add to the WS6 epic AC or 'Specifics pinned' section a one-paragraph processing protocol for the coverage-gap sub-reason derivation that names the exact `verdict.coverage` fields to inspect: `enabled==false` → `gate-disabled`; `llm_unavailable==true` → `llm-unavailable`; `security_detectors` list present with any `reason=='fail-closed-abstain'` entry → `scanner`; exception path in adapter → `review-error`.
findings.finding: The coverage-gap sub-reason derivation rule for the WS6 Gerrit vote message (`gate-disabled` / `llm-unavailable` / `scanner` / `review-error`) is referenced in the epic AC for WS6 ('the documented first-line tag') and in the WS6 child-ticket body, but the classification algorithm — which `verdict.coverage` field values map to which sub-reason — is never specified in the epic plan and is not co-located with the verdict-producing functions (`_inert_code_review_verdict`, `_degraded_code_review_verdict`, `apply_failclosed`) whose output shapes it must classify.
findings.location: WS6 AC (epic plan, section B) / src/rebar/llm/workflow/gate_dispatch.py:366-388 / src/rebar/llm/code_review/detectors.py:74

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `CodeReviewBatchRunner` explicitly to WS3's scope and ACs (or reassign its authorship explicitly to WS4 and remove it from WS4's 'WS3 shipped' prerequisite list).
findings.finding: WS4 describes `CodeReviewBatchRunner` as a WS3-shipped artifact that `produce_code_review_verdict` constructs, but WS3's own ACs and scope make no mention of `CodeReviewBatchRunner`. WS3 defines `overlay_union`, `merge_findings`, `gates/code-review.yaml`, and `workflow_ops.py` — not a named BatchRunner class.
findings.location: child 98ff-d3b8-d2f7-4a86 (WS4) + child 8855-e5f9-05b4-4a04 (WS3)
findings.suggested_fix: Add to WS3's scope: `src/rebar/llm/code_review/batch_runner.py` — `CodeReviewBatchRunner(context: str)` (the per-overlay batch runner injecting diff context, parallel to `ProductionBatchRunner`). Add an AC: 'CodeReviewBatchRunner is importable and tested.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] In the WS6 plan section (or the epic plan's 'Specifics pinned' section), co-locate the `enabled` parameter's processing protocol with the `produce_code_review_verdict` function contract: explicitly state that `enabled=True` short-circuits `code_review_enabled()` entirely (i.e., no config read), `enabled=False` forces inert, and `enabled=None` reads config — and confirm whether `code_review_enabled()` needs a matching `enabled` override.
findings.finding: The `enabled: bool | None = None` override that WS6 AC3 requires on `produce_code_review_verdict` is absent from the shipped function signature (gate_dispatch.py:392), AND the processing protocol for this parameter — specifically the precedence rule when `enabled=True` is passed alongside a `repo_root` whose config has `enable_code_review=false` — is never specified in the epic plan. The child ticket (4119) implies `enabled=True` bypasses config entirely, but that rule is not co-located with `code_review_enabled()` or `produce_code_review_verdict` in any plan section or code docstring.
findings.location: WS6 AC3 / src/rebar/llm/workflow/gate_dispatch.py:392 (produce_code_review_verdict signature)
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the WS6 AC3 or the 'Specifics pinned' section: '`enabled=True` bypasses `code_review_enabled()` entirely (no config read); `enabled=False` forces the inert verdict regardless of config; `enabled=None` (default) reads config via `code_review_enabled(repo_root)`. The `code_review_enabled()` helper is NOT modified — the override short-circuits the call site in `produce_code_review_verdict` directly.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Either add `src/rebar/llm/code_review/contracts.py` explicitly to WS1's scope (creating it with `code_review_base_output` registration), or update WS2 to CREATE `contracts.py` itself (not extend a WS1 file).
findings.finding: WS2 describes `code_review/contracts.py` as 'WS1's module' to extend (registering the `code_review_coach` move-pick contract 'alongside WS1's `code_review_base_output`'), but WS1's scope does not create a `contracts.py` file — WS1 registers the output schema in `src/rebar/schemas/` and the prompt `outputs:` binding, with no mention of a `contracts.py` module.
findings.location: child 8e78-d515-3a98-4eba (WS2) + child 8e6f-3ad2-1f24-4b8b (WS1)
findings.suggested_fix: Add to WS1's scope: 'src/rebar/llm/code_review/contracts.py — create with the `code_review_base_output` structured-output contract registration.' WS2 then correctly extends an existing file.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Remove the duplicate 'Integration shape pinned' paragraph in the 'Decisions pinned' secrets/security bullet, retaining only the second (more complete) instance.
findings.finding: The 'Integration shape pinned by derisk experiment (Round-11; session_log a7e2-381c-6dc6-4d0c)' paragraph appears twice in the same bullet under 'Decisions pinned (2026-06)', producing a near-verbatim duplicate block that is likely to confuse implementers about which version is authoritative.
findings.location: Decisions pinned (2026-06) — 'Secrets/security detector path (v1 default)' bullet, second sub-block starting 'Integration shape pinned by derisk experiment'
findings.suggested_fix: Delete the first occurrence of the 'Integration shape pinned' sub-block (it ends with '...which also serves semgrep and any future SARIF tool. Fail-CLOSED lives...'); keep the second, which contains the same content plus the explicit semgrep/gitleaks distinction.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Remove one of the two identical 'WS6 staged rollout (Round-11)' bullets in 'Specifics pinned'.
findings.finding: The bullet 'WS6 staged rollout (Round-11): the Gerrit voter activates behind a per-project/staged opt-in...' appears twice in the 'Specifics pinned (plan-review remediation 2026-06)' section — verbatim duplication of the same requirement.
findings.location: Specifics pinned (plan-review remediation 2026-06) — two consecutive bullets both beginning 'WS6 staged rollout (Round-11)'

findings.finding: Acceptance-criteria / definition-of-done quality issues.
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] WS6 AC: explicitly specify that the review_bot's Gerrit HTTP password and LLM API keys in the AWS deployment are injected via a secrets manager (AWS Secrets Manager or SSM Parameter Store), not committed or baked into env vars in source.
findings.finding: The plan never specifies how the deployed `review_bot`'s runtime credentials (Gerrit HTTP password, LLM API keys wired in WS6) are stored and injected in the AWS environment. The single reference to credentials is the `gerrit_client.py` seam from d251, but WS6 adds a new LLM provider credential path and there is no mention of SSM Parameter Store, AWS Secrets Manager, or any vault.
findings.location: WS6 AC — 'REWIRE d251's shipped review_bot.adapter.code_review_decision over produce_code_review_verdict'; Dependencies section — 'd251 shipped src/rebar/review_bot/… gerrit_client.py'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a WS6 AC line: 'Runtime credentials (Gerrit HTTP password, LLM provider API key) are stored in AWS Secrets Manager / SSM SecureString and injected at runtime; no plaintext credential appears in source, config files, or container image layers.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a brief prior-art note for the reviewer architecture (analogous to 8f6c's buy-vs-build section) documenting whether existing agentic code-review tools with LLM specialist-routing (e.g. CodeRabbit, Reviewpad, GitHub Copilot code review) were surveyed as potential adapters before committing to the bespoke two-round escalation design, and why integration with an existing tool was ruled out.
findings.finding: The plan commits to a novel LLM-specialist-routing architecture (base reviewer + two-round overlay escalation) without documenting whether existing agentic code-review tools that already implement comparable specialist-routing were surveyed and ruled out as adapters or integration targets. The d251 prior-art research covers only the Gerrit voting-shape (external-bot model, citing amarula/reviewai); no equivalent survey appears for the review architecture itself — analogous to 8f6c's explicit 'no reusable WHOLE exists' prior-art statement for the oracle layer.
findings.location: Design section — 'four-pass consumer + base->overlay escalation (mechanism PROVEN on the v3 engine)'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a 'Why not an existing agentic code-review tool?' paragraph in the Design section or ADR 0010, mirroring 8f6c's framing: e.g. 'Commercial tools (CodeRabbit, Reviewpad, GitHub Copilot code review) are closed-SaaS, emit their own verdict formats, and cannot consume the shared four-pass kernel or the grounding oracle's fail-open evidence model — so a bespoke kernel-consuming gate was the only path to the recall/precision split and dogfood posture this plan requires.'

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
